summary: Gruber explains that he sometimes writes articles not for his regular Daring Fireball audience, but specifically for search engine discoverability. After publishing a Mac OS X troubleshooting article, it became the top Google result within five days, helping strangers solve their problems. He offers practical tips for writing search-friendly content: use descriptive titles matching likely search queries, and front-load factual details in the opening paragraphs. He acknowledges that Google's indexer might be smart enough to surface the right results regardless, but argues the approach is worth trying.
key_points: Gruber distinguishes between writing for his regular audience and writing for search engine discovery, treating Google as 'the world's best troubleshooting database'
His troubleshooting article reached the #1 Google result within five days, validating the approach
Practical SEO advice: use clear, query-matching titles and front-load factual content in early paragraphs
The real payoff is helping strangers who find the article through search — one reader was saved from a needless OS reinstall
money_quotes: Blog it so it gets into Google, the world's best troubleshooting database.
But it's only funny because it's true.
They were written for Google. Or, more specifically, for people using search engines to find an answer to the same problems I had.
If you happened to enjoy them anyway, well, that's just gravy.
Don't get cute with the title.
The point is, get to the point quickly.
It's possible that Google's indexer is so smart that it would still provide the same search results, even if I had titled the article 'The .GlobalPreferences.plist File Can Bite Me'.
key_insight: The most valuable blog posts aren't always written for your regular audience — sometimes you write for the stranger desperately searching Google for the exact problem you just solved.
